sentences.text: In Germany, in a place we were given-- after I was out from Sankt Ottilien after a year, we were placed in houses in Germany.
And the houses belonged before to big SS people.
So I was placed in a villa.
It was called Villa [?
Meindl ?] in [?
Doffenmarkt. ?] And a Mr.
Meindl ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was still in France in prison because he was a high-ranked SS man.
So we were in that beautiful villa.
It has a swimming pool, with all the comforts.
And we were about 25 young boys and girls.
And that villa was transformed to a school of agriculture because we were preparing to go all to Israel.
So they had a teacher.
And he was teaching us agriculture.
So when we come to Israel in the kibbutz, we'll know a little about agriculture.
So we stayed in that villa for quite-- almost a year.
Did you have any choice in where you were sent at this point?
No, I didn't.
The Sankt Ottiliens, they, they had already a list from the American government or from the German government.
The other is empty homes.
There are-- no Germans are there.
It's locked up.
And there was a lot of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people placed in these homes.
I was with my brother.
And then, when my sister came back from Lithuania, she came right in-- into that Villa [?
Meindl. ?] So we were all three.
Only my oldest sister was still living in Italy.
She was married already in Italy with her husband.
And she had a baby there in-- after a year, or a year later in Italy.
With my sister?
Yeah, I was in touch when she left Italy.
And she lived in Canada.
And I was in the United States.
So I traveled.
And we met in Canada with my sister.
Yeah, we were writing letters.
Yes.
Yeah, the girls had a separate rooms.
And the boys had separate rooms.
Because it was a very big villa.
It looked like-- to compare, in the United States, a mansion like that runs, like, almost a million dollars.
We were served, all, you know, American-supplied foods, almost the same thing like the American soldiers or captains or majors eating.
They were supplied to that hospital all foods because in that hospital was kitchens and everything.
And the Germans had to supply with foods, too, towards that hospital.
Yes, there was a rabbi.
It was all made kosher.
Yeah, that was kashrut.
To me, personally it was not important, no.
I am a very good Jew.
But I'm not-- I'm ashamed-- [LAUGHS] I should be ashamed.
I'm not too religious.
I don't eat directly pork or thing.
But if a can beans from American soldier is not kosher, I'm not going to make a big, you know, fuss about it.
I'll eat it with pleasure.
Oh yeah, there was people who ate only kosher.
The same thing, like-- you take now an airplane, you go to Israel.
They ask you, you want kosher food or the regular?
The religious people says they want it kosher.
They get kosher.
No.
I wouldn't want to see my hometown.
My future was to go to the United States.
I was dreaming I about Israel-- to go.
But I figured I have in the United States my father's brothers.
I have two uncles and a aunt.
And I figured if I'm not going to like it, I have all the time, time to go to Israel.
Yes.
I discussed-- my brother decided to go to Israel.
And my sister, Miriam, decided to go to Israel, too.
So they went to Israel.
Yeah, it was very hard [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] separation.
But I knew we are not going in concentration camps.
[LAUGHS] We can all the time meet-- anytime we want, we can meet.
I was at that time already an old man, 21 years.
Yes.
After I was in Sankt Ottilien, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two, I think, majors-- American majors in a jeep arrived in Sankt Ottilien.

And they told the chief, you know, from the hospitals that they got to interview people who were in Dachau because there's going to start a trial against 40 the biggest Nazis who carried on in Dachau, the commandant with all the big shots, the murderers.
And there's going to start a trial.
And they need important witnesses who saw, who lived, who knew certain things and can speak out, you know.
So they start to interview a lot of people, you know, in different-- first, in different DP camps and in hospitals.
And then they came to my hospital in Sankt Ottilien.
So they interviewed one room, another room, another room.
Then they ask voluntarily, who wants to come for interview to be a witness in Dachau?
I said, I was in Dachau.
I want to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would like to be interviewed.
It would be my biggest pleasure.
And I hate to say that word, 'revenge.' But I must say that would be the best thing in my life if I can meet eye-on-eye with the commandant from Dachau and look him in the eyes.
That would be-- people were wishing in the camps just to punch one of these guys in the nose and then die.
That kind of wish they used they have in the camps.
And I have the opportunity like that, if I can-- if I'm going to be, be able to go through with the interview and be selected, I would-- I would thank God.
I would fall on my knees.
So I was interviewed by the two-- I think they were, if I don't mistake-- captains, maybe.
There are two, here-- maybe the captains.
And they were, like, assistant, you know, prosecutors to the American, you know, tribunal.
Because in Dachau, the trial was not by civilians.
It was by the American government.
It was American Army-- government trial, you know.
So they start to ask me certain questions.
And I-- the last three, four months in Dachau, I was working already in the barrack their mechanics, you know, work in German mechanics.
We are building, you know, for the Messerschmitt, all kind of munitions, you know, and parts.

Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were building-- they, they were all the time saying, the Germans, in that place, it used to be called, 'Moll.' Moll was called that place where they were building the big factories.
They said, here, we are going to build the secret weapon.
And they are going to wipe out America to the ground.
That's what used to say every day the Germans to me because I was working in the same place where they were working.
They needed a woodturner.
One time they came while we were-- came from the camp.
And we are still in the lines.
Germans run through.
And they called, is somebody a woodturner?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Run, run, run, run.
And then they came to my section where I was.
We just came to work.
And we were still, you know, standing, you know, in the lines, five at line.
Is somebody a woodturner?
So I raised my hand.
I'm a woodturner.
So he looked at me.
He says, if you are not a woodturner, you're going to be shot on the place.
Because he couldn't believe, you know, a young boy like me is a woodturner.
Most woodturners takes a lot of experience.
And most of them are 40- and 50-year-old men.
So he looked at me again.
And he says-- in German, he says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]?
I said no-- if I'm lying.
I said, no, I'm a woodturner.
So he took me out from the line.
And he took me in right away in a place with a roof under my head in a machine shop.
And that was life.
Because before, all these months, we were working outside in the rain, snow, cold.
We were shaking and shivering.
And all of a sudden, I am taken in, you know, in that thing.
And he took me.
And he gave me a little lathe.
And he says, turn.
And he gave me a little paper.
I sketched, like, a blueprint and a certain part, maybe it goes to machinery what they were building, you know.
And I turned it out.
And he said to me-- in German, he said, I cannot believe it.
Jew knows how to turn wood.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he gave me the place to work.
So while I was working in that place, I used to come in contact with a lot of the Germans, you know.
The big shots used to come in, too, you know, and yakety-yak, you know, with the big engineers there about-- talking what they're building.
Maybe they are-- they're going to build, you know, the big secret weapon, what they called 'atom bomb.'
And I saw, you know, the terrible things, you know, while they were talking, the, the killings and all the things.
So the Americans, while they interviewed me, they saw that I am good material to testify.
And I saw a lot of people getting killed with my own eyes by these people-- and their names, the people who got killed, and when.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Excuse me.
So they selected me.
And they said to me, we're going to pick you up.
And they give you the date.
And then two weeks later, they came again, and again interviewed me again and looked in the paper to see, you know, if I'll make a witness not to get, you know, confused.
And if so, that everything I told them before-- it's almost like you cannot forget troubles when it's in your brain, understand?
It shows that everything is intact.
They says, oh, this is going to be a witness.
He's the youngest witness-- going to be.
Because most of the witnesses were doctors, lawyers.
They were prisoners, priests.
But a young boy like me, I felt funny, you know, that I'm going to be a witness, too, between all the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] intellectual, you know, educated, you know, prominent ex-prisoners.
So it took again maybe three weeks or something like that.
They came.
And they told the people in the hospital they were taking away Moses to the-- Dachau.
And I think he's going to stay maybe there five or six weeks.
And they took me to Dachau.
And they gave me a room.
And I was in Dachau in a private room.
And a MP was standing by my door day and night while I was there and watching me-- nothing should happen to me.
And sometimes the MP and the SS, who used to be my guards around the fence when I was in Dachau used to clean my room.
So the satisfaction was so big.
And if I had sometime a MP man who knew a little how to talk Polish or Jewish or Russian, he used to say to me, if you want to kick him in the behind, I don't see nothing.
He says, I didn't see nothing.
I didn't hear nothing.
You can kick him.
So sometimes the American MP used to throw the cigarette out on the floor so the SS man was going to pick up.
So he winked with his eye-- now is good to give a kick the man.
You'll get a little of your anger out.
So-- but who had a-- Moses the killer, me?
[LAUGHS] It's not in my blood.
I gave him a little touch.
Let me ask just a couple little questions.
These pre-interviews-- the interviews--
Yes.
The pre-interview, it was in, in German, in German.
These two captains, you know, the Americans, they knew German.
They spoke German perfect.
From the hospital, they took Dr.
Burman, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Dr.
Katz, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and Dr.
Kaufman.
Dr.
Katz, I think is Jonas, [?
Jona, ?] [?
Jona ?] Katz.
And Burman-- I don't know the first name.
We didn't call them-- we used to call them Dr.
Kaufman, [LAUGHS] like your name, Kaufman.
Yeah, Dr.
Kaufman, Dr.
Katz, and Dr.
Burman.
Oh, it was witnesses not from the hospital.
It was witnesses from all over-- from different places.
It, it was picked back-- witnesses came from England back.
It was a case, understand, there that a German woman came-- a free woman.
She was-- came from outside from Dachau, living free.
But in the wartime, she was a secretary to a big, you know, Nazi, you know.
She was a secretary.
And she was said-- I, I don't-- I believe it was [?
Frischnell, ?] if, if I don't mistake.
And when the Americans or English soldiers were shot down in Germany, they used to bring to that German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to find out, you know, all the things and interrogate them.
And she used to type on the machine.
And she wanted to make herself like she's a still a good woman, and she's going to help in the process in the trial to get a good word against that big Nazi.
So she was called on the stand.
They asked her, what are talking-- what are you saying about your boss?
Did he ever beat, you know, American or English prisoners who were shot down by the airplanes?
She says, oh no.
He was just questioning them and just, you know, all questions.
He never laid a hand on a prisoner.
And then, after she was testifying, right away they called an English man.
And he came out here.
He was in England maybe a year after the liberation.
So he came with his uniform.
And they called him to the stand.
And they said to the woman, do you recognize that man?
Did you ever see him?
She says, no, I've never seen him.
And that English man start-- they asked him, did you ever see that woman?
He says, of course I saw her.
Then her boss knocked out a teeth while questioning me.
All the blood fell on her typewriter.
And she said, in German, that dirty-- [SPEAKING GERMAN].
That means that 'dirty English man with his dirty blood.
Look what he made on my typewriter.' And he told the whole story.
And while she came a free person to testify for her boss-- because she swear by God to tell nothing but the truth.
And she was lying right there.
They arrested her.
[LAUGHTER]
So that was-- all kind of incident was on trial.
So it was not only, you know, from-- people from Dachau.
It came-- girls from Poland were brought who were, you know, experimented in a cold, ice swimming pool.
They were thrown in and to-- with other people to see how long they can stay in ice.
And, like, 10 other people, they all died.
Only the two, they're still barely alive.
And they were placed naked between two German soldiers to body-- warm them up to see, experimenting.
And the professor didn't believe that these Polish girls are going to be brought from, from Poland to be on trial.
And that was very unbelievable moments like that.
The professor was-- his name was Professor Schilling.
And if people know medicine-- especially doctors must know-- that professor was one of the first professors who invented the [GERMAN] that you taken blood from your finger with the needle, that test.
It's called a test of [GERMAN] in German.
In English, the doctors know what it means when you take from your finger the blood.
That Professor Schilling, he was the-- one of the inventors who invented the analysis of the [GERMAN] test.
And that professor made all kind of tests on human beings.
He was a old man, almost-- [SIGHS] maybe about 75 years, with a long, little beard, very frail, skinny-looking.
And he was then called to the stand after the girls came, you know, and told what happened to them.
They called the professor.
He was between the 40 criminals on the stand.
He was called.
And he says, yeah, he did make the experiments because he wanted for the world the health, and for life to improve.
That's why he was experimenting.
No.
If I would be in contact with him, maybe I wouldn't have been alive.
Because most people who were in contact with that man, they never saw them again.
Yeah, I, I saw one time they took a priest out in the middle of the night.
And he was laying right not far from me.
And he said to me-- and I was-- that time, I was sick with my pleurisy.
And he was a little sick, too.
I don't know what sickness it was.
But he was, like, laying right close by me.
And when they took him out in the middle of the night, he touched me on the shoulder.
He said, Moses, God help you.
I don't think you'll see again.
But God knows everything.
He never came back.
He must've taken him away for experiments.
The trial began, I think, November 15, if I don't mistake, 1945.
Yes.
I bought-- I was brought with American, with MP.
No.
I could have-- if I wanted, I couldn't have been after I testified.
I could have gone away.
But I was all the time because it was so suspense what you saw and what people, they're telling their stories-- that I thought my stories was big.
My story was-- comparing with other stories, was very little, minor.
The courtroom was a very big room.
I think that was the entertainment hall for the SS, I think.
And when the Americans took over, they made it a courtroom.
It was a tremendous, big hall.

I, as a mechanic, I would say it must have been [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about 150 feet or 200 feet by 500 feet, long hall with-- all set up like regular big courtrooms, you know, like for-- almost like a Nuremberg Trial.
No, we were sitting right close, like-- almost like in the movie, we were sitting.
The matter of fact, on the trial was even the relatives from the criminals who got sentence, all from the 40 criminals who were sentenced-- or going to be sentenced.
Their wives, their brothers, or relatives-- they're in the courtroom.

Because not far from me on the third seat, they are sitting, [LAUGHS] by coincidence-- the commandant that-- Johann Kirsch from my camp, his wife, and some relatives, maybe sons or something like that.
Because I heard.
While we were brought in, the MP guided us.
Go in that row.
Go in that row.
You know, like, like a usher, almost, in the movies.
So I was not far, you know, from the family, like, two seats.
And I was hearing what she was talking in German to her-- to her son, or somebody.
And she was holding her hand like that.
My poor, my, my [GERMAN] Johann.
My [GERMAN] Johann, you know.
[LAUGHS] So-- and she was telling him all these stories, you know, these [? hand. ?] Do you remember any of that?
Yes, I couldn't concentrate, you know, because I was listening more.
But I heard a couple of sentences.
And I knew that's the family.
Oh, it was press from all over the world.
The matter of fact, on the third day of the trial, a French [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] journalist had the permission to come into my room where I was sleeping.
And he had a little bag.
And he opened up the bag, and he had a suit.
And he said, Moses, I want to give you this suit for a gift.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because I want something to do.
And I don't have-- that's the only thing.
I have four or five suits.
And it was almost my height.
And he says, I know it's going to fit you.
And he gave me the suit.
I said, thank you, thank you.
And then he wanted to interview me, you know.
I said, maybe another day.
